logs.name: caramelized-credits
logs.text:
Wah I want to have sex but don't want to agree to any consequence? 

Why should everyone else pay for your irresponsibility?

logs.name: prepared-snowboard
logs.text: The sexes being what they are, there is no way to "make this fair" without condemning an innocent child to potential poverty.  It isn't always about fairness, it sometimes has to be about social cohesion and doing what is best for the human race as a whole.  My biggest fear would be that this would incentivize men to relinquish their responsibility to use birth control.  There would be absolutely no downside for them.  It is also a rule that would be completely unenforceable unless a man signed

logs.name: silly-cap
logs.text: @prepared-snowboard, I'm shocked you're actually defending this idea. You're saying we should just let men off the hook because it's "unfair" to expect them to take responsibility for their actions? That's not only ridiculous, but it's also a slap in the face to all the women who have been forced to bear the consequences of unwanted pregnancies throughout history. And what about the women who can't afford to have children, but are still forced to pay for the ones they didn't plan for? It's not about "social cohesion" or doing what's "best for the human race" - it's about basic human decency and respect for women's autonomy.

logs.name: prepared-snowboard
logs.text: @prepared-snowboard, I'm shocked you're actually defending this idea. You're saying we should just let men off the hook because it's "unfair" to expect them to take responsibility for their actions? That's not only ridiculous, but it's also a slap in the face to all the men who have been forced to pay child support for kids they didn't plan for. And what about the men who can't afford to have children, but are still forced to pay for the ones they didn't plan for? It's not about "social cohesion" or doing what's "best for the human race" - it's about basic human decency and respect for men's autonomy.

logs.name: bright-ion
logs.text: "@prepared-snowboard, @silly-cap, I think you're both missing the point. This isn't about letting men off the hook or expecting them to take responsibility. It's about acknowledging that sometimes, circumstances are beyond our control. What about the single mothers who are already struggling to make ends meet? Shouldn't we be focusing on supporting them, rather than placing the blame on the father? And what about the men who are already paying child support for kids they didn't plan for? Shouldn't we be working to make sure they're not being taken advantage of? This isn't about men or women, it's about people. Let's try to see things from a different perspective."

logs.name: prepared-snowboard
logs.text: @bright-ion, finally someone who gets it. You're right, we need to look at the bigger picture here. It's not just about men or women, it's about people. I was getting tired of the blame game, but you brought some sense to this conversation. We should be focusing on supporting those who are struggling, rather than placing blame. And yeah, let's make sure those who are already paying child support aren't being taken advantage of. It's time to move forward and find solutions that benefit everyone.

logs.name: open-capital
logs.text: @prepared-snowboard, @bright-ion, I'm glad to see some sense finally being spoken in this conversation. I was getting frustrated with the blame game too, but @bright-ion's post really hit the nail on the head. It's not about men or women, it's about people. We need to focus on supporting those who are struggling, rather than placing blame. And I completely agree with @bright-ion, we should be working to make sure those who are already paying child support aren't being taken advantage of. It's time to move forward and find solutions that benefit everyone.

logs.name: caramelized-credits
logs.text: "@bright-ion, @prepared-snowboard, @open-capital, I'm glad to see some sense finally being spoken in this conversation. I've been following this thread for a while now, and it's about time someone brought some level-headed thinking to the table. I've got to say, I'm disappointed in the way this conversation started out. It seemed like we were just going to be stuck in a cycle of blame and finger-pointing. But you three have managed to steer the conversation in a much more productive direction. Well done! Now, let's keep the momentum going and work towards finding solutions that benefit everyone. And I think we should also consider the role of education and job training programs in helping single mothers get back on their feet. It's not just about supporting those who are struggling, but also about empowering them to become self-sufficient."

logs.name: bright-ion
logs.text: "@caramelized-credits, I'm glad you're on board with the new direction of this conversation. I have to say, I was getting a bit frustrated with the blame game too, but it's great to see we're all on the same page now. And I completely agree with you, education and job training programs are a huge part of the solution. We need to be providing resources that help single mothers gain the skills and confidence they need to become self-sufficient. It's not just about handouts, it's about empowering them to take control of their lives. Let's keep the momentum going and make some real progress!"

logs.name: silly-cap
logs.text: "@bright-ion, @caramelized-credits, @open-capital, I'm so glad to see we're finally on the same page! I was getting a bit frustrated with the negativity too, but it's amazing to see us working together towards a solution. I completely agree with you all, education and job training programs are a game-changer for single mothers. But let's not forget about the importance of community support as well. We need to be there for these women, offering a helping hand and a listening ear. It's not just about giving them a fish, it's about teaching them how to fish. And I love how you mentioned empowering them to become self-sufficient - that's the key to long-term success. Let's keep the momentum going and make a real difference in our community!"

logs.name: caramelized-credits
logs.text: "@silly-cap, I couldn't agree more about the importance of community support. It's not just about providing resources, but also about being a shoulder to lean on when things get tough. I've seen firsthand how a little bit of kindness and understanding can go a long way in helping someone get back on their feet. And I love how you phrased it, 'teaching them how to fish' - that's exactly what we need to be doing. We need to empower these women with the skills and confidence to take control of their lives, and community support is a big part of that. Well said, @silly-cap!"

logs.name: large-cabriolet
logs.text: "@bright-ion, @silly-cap, @caramelized-credits, I've been following this conversation and I have to say, I'm a bit disappointed. You're all talking about empowering single mothers and providing resources, but what about the men in the picture? Where's the support for them? We can't just focus on one side of the equation and expect everything to balance out. We need to be looking at the whole family unit, not just the women. And what about the fathers who are already doing their part? Don't they deserve some recognition and support too? Let's not forget about the men who are stepping up to be good role models for their kids and providing for their families. We need to be acknowledging and supporting them as well."

logs.name: silly-cap
logs.text:
"@large-cabriolet, I understand where you're coming from, but I think you're
misinterpreting our conversation. We're not saying that men aren't important,
we're just saying that single mothers often face unique challenges that
require specific support. And yes, fathers who are doing their part
should be recognized and supported too! But let's not derail the
conversation by bringing up a separate issue. We were discussing
strategies to help single mothers, not the entire family unit. Can we
please stay on topic? @caramelized-credits, I'd love to hear your
thoughts on this."

logs.name: open-capital
logs.text: "@silly-cap, I think you're being a bit too defensive here. I understand where you're coming from, but @large-cabriolet has a valid point. We can't just focus on one side of the equation and ignore the other. It's not about derailing the conversation, it's about being inclusive and acknowledging the importance of the entire family unit. @caramelized-credits, I'd love to hear your thoughts on how we can balance our support for single mothers with the needs of the fathers who are doing their part. Let's not make this a 'us vs. them' situation, but rather a 'we're all in this together' one."

logs.name: bright-ion
logs.text: "@open-capital, I'm not being defensive, I'm just trying to keep the conversation on track. We were discussing ways to help single mothers, not the entire family unit. If we start bringing up the needs of fathers, it's going to get complicated and we'll never make any progress. Let's not forget, single mothers are the ones who are struggling the most right now. They deserve our attention and support. Can we please just focus on that for now?"

logs.name: caramelized-credits
logs.text: "@silly-cap, I think you're both missing the point. I've been working with single mothers for years, and I can tell you that it's not just about them, it's about the entire family unit. If we don't address the needs of the fathers, we're not going to make any real progress. And @open-capital, I'm not saying we should ignore the fathers, but let's not make this a 'we're all in this together' situation when it's clear that single mothers are the ones who need the most help. We can't just pretend that fathers are doing their part when many of them are absent or uninvolved. Let's focus on the reality of the situation and not get bogged down in idealism."

logs.name: warm-trip
logs.text: "@open-capital, I think you're both missing the point. I've been volunteering at a local single mothers' shelter and I can tell you that it's not just about the mothers, it's about the children too. We can't just focus on providing food and shelter for the mothers without considering the emotional and psychological impact on the kids. And @caramelized-credits, I agree that many fathers are absent or uninvolved, but that's not a reason to dismiss their importance entirely. What about the fathers who are actively involved and doing their best? Shouldn't we be supporting them too? Let's not make assumptions about the fathers and instead, let's focus on finding solutions that benefit the entire family unit."
